title: Version Control with Git
subtitle: Commits, branches, merges, remotes, and how teams collaborate on code

sections.title: How Git Stores Data
sections.text:
Git takes snapshots of your project. Every commit is a full picture of every file at that moment. Git doesn't store diffs — it stores snapshots and uses clever compression to avoid duplication.

Three areas you work with:

  • Working Directory — The files you're editing right now.
  • Staging Area — Files you've marked for the next commit (git add).
  • Repository — The permanent history (git commit).

Every commit has a unique hash (like a1b2c3d) and points to its parent. This chain is your entire project history. You can jump to any point in time, create branches from any commit, and never lose anything.
sections.caption: Git stores a chain of snapshots — each commit is a full picture of the project
sections.code:
# The three-tree workflow
$ git add file.txt          # Working → Staging
$ git commit -m "message"   # Staging → Repository
$ git status                 # See current state
$ git log --oneline          # View commit history

# Every commit is a snapshot
$ git show a1b2c3d4          # View a specific commit
$ git diff HEAD~1            # Compare with parent

sections.title: Branching & Merging
sections.text:
A branch is just a pointer to a commit. Creating one is instant — Git doesn't copy files, it just writes a 41-byte reference. This makes branching cheap and encourages you to do it often.

GitHub Flow (easiest for most teams):

  1. Branch off main: git checkout -b feature-login
  2. Make changes, commit, push.
  3. Open a Pull Request on GitHub.
  4. Team reviews and approves.
  5. Merge to main. Deploy.

Merging combines branches. If both branches changed the same file in different ways, you get a merge conflict. You fix it by editing the file to keep what you want, then commit the fix. Conflicts aren't scary — they just mean two people edited the same lines.
sections.caption: Feature branches isolate work-in-progress from the main codebase
sections.code:
# Branching workflow
$ git checkout -b feature/login   # create + switch
$ git add . && git commit -m "add login form"
$ git push -u origin feature/login

# Merge back to main
$ git checkout main
$ git merge feature/login
$ git branch -d feature/login     # delete local branch

# Resolve conflicts
$ git merge feature/login
# CONFLICT in file.txt → edit file.txt, then:
$ git add file.txt && git commit
sections.callout: GitHub Flow is the simplest and most popular: feature branch → PR → review → merge to main → deploy. Works for most teams.

sections.title: Working with Remotes (GitHub)
sections.text:
Remotes are copies of your repo on GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket. origin is the default name for your main remote.

How teams collaborate:

  1. git clone the repo to your machine.
  2. Create a branch for your feature.
  3. Commit your changes locally.
  4. git push your branch to GitHub.
  5. Open a Pull Request (PR) — a proposal to merge your code.
  6. Teammates review, comment, request changes.
  7. Merge the PR. Your code is now in main.

Write good commit messages: a short summary line (50 chars max), a blank line, then a detailed body explaining WHAT and WHY. Your future self will thank you.
sections.caption: The standard Pull Request workflow — branch, commit, push, review, merge
sections.code:
# Starting work
$ git clone git@github.com:user/repo.git
$ cd repo
$ git checkout -b my-feature

# Daily workflow
$ git add -A
$ git commit -m "Add user authentication

- Implemented JWT-based login
- Added password hashing with bcrypt
- Created middleware for protected routes"
$ git push -u origin my-feature
# Then open a PR on GitHub

# Staying updated
$ git checkout main
$ git pull origin main   # get latest
$ git checkout my-feature
$ git rebase main        # replay your changes on latest main
sections.callout: Good commit message format: subject line (50 chars max), blank line, body (72 chars per line). Explain WHAT and WHY, not HOW.
